headline: Iran Strikes Reload Brent War Premium
deck: Saudi Arabia cut official selling prices as OPEC+ cleared a fifth straight monthly output rise and Brent settled Monday at $71.99. Attacks on vessels near the strait then put 61 cents back on the barrel and reopened a war premium the market had spent three weeks fading. The sub-$70 Friday call, once priced at 0.62, now carries probability 0.25.

art.caption: Two wars price one barrel: drones subtract refining, missiles tax the strait, OPEC+ ships into the middle

body: A missile beat the sub-$70 oil call before Tuesday's open could settle it. On Sunday OPEC+ agreed a fifth straight monthly output rise and Saudi Arabia cut official selling prices, and Brent settled Monday at $71.99, down 0.2% on the session [E1]. That settlement was the glut tape the forecast had been built on: a sub-$70 settle by Friday, once priced YES at 0.62. Supply was winning the argument on paper.

Overnight attacks on vessels near the Strait of Hormuz put 61 cents back on the barrel by late morning, lifting Brent to $72.60 by 1140 GMT [E3]. UKMTO recorded a tanker hit eight nautical miles east of Limah at 2119 UTC on 6 July, struck by an unknown projectile [E7]. Something larger returned with the cents: the war premium the market had spent three weeks unwinding. Fear repriced faster than the extra barrels could absorb.

With Brent at $72.60 and the risk premium reloading [E3], the probability of a sub-$70 settle by Friday's window collapses to p=0.25. The original call stands until Friday's settlement scores it, and a miss prints to the ledger. The reversal is stated here plainly, not buried beneath fresh headlines. Deadline and probability travel together on the forecast label.

Beneath the fear, the curve still argues glut. Brent futures carry mild contango through the winter months, with September at 72.09, November at 72.43, and February 2027 fading to 71.84 [E4]. Prompt supply had already tipped the structure into contango by 3 July, before the strikes interrupted the unwind [E5]. The shape is a market that believes in surplus just underneath the premium spike. Calendar spreads have not yet surrendered to panic.

Refined products stayed firm against the crude slide. August ULSD traded at $3.2371 per gallon, up 1.73% on the day [E6]. Eight OPEC+ producers confirmed an August output adjustment of 188,000 barrels per day [E2]. New barrels sail directly into the middle of a barrel already pulled apart by geography. Products strength suggests demand for distillates has not followed crude lower.

Ukraine's drone campaign keeps subtracting Russian refining capacity even as Iran's missiles tax traffic through the strait. The Omsk refinery fire sits on the same supply map as the tanker warnings off Limah: one conflict erodes throughput inland, the other threatens the export lane. Russia's refining complex continues to lose barrels that might otherwise reach export markets. The split persists across the barrel's two hemispheres of risk.

Saxo's Ole Hansen priced the next escalation rungs at $75 and $80 should strait risk deepen further [E3]. OPEC+'s August barrels arrive into that tension, not around it. Two wars now price the same barrel from opposite ends while fresh supply sails into the middle. The desk sized the glut correctly and the geopolitics incorrectly. Friday's settlement will print which force the market weighed heavier.
